reasoning: A pratyāhāra spans from its first sound up to (but not including) the marker; iK covers i through the marker K, giving i u ṛ ḷ.
haṆ starts at h and ends just before the next Ṇ (after l), yielding h y v r l.

answer: kh ph ch ṭh th c ṭ t k p
reasoning: khaY begins at kh and runs to before Y (after p), so it includes kh ph ch ṭh th c ṭ t k p.
The nasals are the run from ñ to the marker M in line 7; the pratyāhāra is formed as ña + M.
The diphthongs are ai au, which are from ai up to marker C.
All sounds are from the first sound a up to before the final marker L.

answer: Name the class by writing the first sound and the ending marker; it denotes all sounds from that start up to (but not including) that marker in the Śivasūtras.
reasoning: This describes how pratyāhāras are built from the ordered list with terminal markers.

answer: Replace i u ṛ ḷ by y v r l respectively when followed by any vowel.
reasoning: iKaḥ yaṆ aCi means the class iK becomes the corresponding sounds of yaṆ before aC (vowels).
In the rules, -aḥ marks the target undergoing substitution.
The empty ending marks the substitute itself, effectively read as ‘by/with’.
The -i ending introduces the conditioning environment to the right.
t (a jhal) becomes d (jaś) before dh (jhaś): jagat + dhana → jagaddhana.
t (jhal) voices to d before d (jhaś): tat + duḥkha → tadduḥkha.
No jhal is immediately before a jhaś in bhrātṛnāman, so the rule does not apply.
